title: Longitudinal Use Patterns of Technology Subtypes During the Transition Into Early Adolescence: Results From the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
journal: The Journal of adolescent health : official publication of the Society for Adolescent Medicine
abstract: PURPOSE: Adolescents encounter a complex digital environment, yet existing data on youth technology use rarely differentiates technology subtypes. This study maps the evolution and intricacies of youth engagement with technology subtypes. METHODS: NÂ = 11,868 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study followed from ages â¼9/10 to â¼13/14. We examined youths' self-reported hours per day (hr/day) of technology subtypes: TV/Movies, video games, YouTube, social media, video chat, and texting. We used descriptive statistics and multilevel logistic regression to assess cross-sectional and longitudinal use patterns of technology subtypes, agreement between child and parent reports on the child's technology use, and associations between each technology subtype and sociodemographics (child's biological sex, parent education, income, and marital status). RESULTS: At age 9/10, â¼75% of youth reported minimal (<30Â min/day) social technology use (social media, video chat, texting) and up to â¼1.5Â hr/day of TV, video games, and YouTube. By age 13/14, TV trajectories were converging to >2Â hr/day, but social technology trajectories "fanned out" into a wide range of usage rates. Child and parent reports were weakly correlated (rs range: 0.13-0.29). Using child-reported hours of technology use, increases in the subject-specific odds of using a technology >2Â hr/day ranged from 25% (YouTube; 95% CI: 1.16-1.35) to 234% (social media; 95% CI: 3.14-3.55). Compared with males, females had â¼100-200% greater odds of >2Â hr/day of social technologies, but â¼40-80% reduced odds of >2Â hr/day of video games and YouTube. Higher parent education and income predicted significantly lower odds of >2Â hr/day of use - regardless of technology subtype. DISCUSSION: Distributions of youths' self-reported technology engagement are highly contingent on technology subtype, age, and biological sex. Future research on youth development and technology may benefit from considering youths' varied digital experiences.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'N = 11,868 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study followed from ages ~9/10 to ~13/14.' Methods state 'We used data from the ABCD longitudinal cohort study. ABCD study recruited youth and parents at 21 United States sites.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple specific smartphone and social media variables: 'We examined youths' self-reported hours per day (hr/day) of technology subtypes: TV/Movies, video games, YouTube, social media, video chat, and texting.' Social media specifically measured as 'social media' outcome; texting on cell phone; video chat; YouTube (all phone-related).

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports multilevel logistic regression and correlation tests linking technology subtypes to sociodemographic outcomes: 'Using child-reported hours of technology use, increases in the subject-specific odds of using a technology >2 hr/day ranged from 25% (YouTube; 95% CI: 1.16–1.35) to 234% (social media; 95% CI: 3.14–3.55).' Also reports associations with biological sex, parental education, income, marital status via multilevel logistic regression.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics reported: ORs with 95% CIs (e.g., 'social media; 95% CI: 3.14–3.55'), correlation coefficients (rs range: 0.13–0.29), test statistics throughout Table 2.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: 'We used multilevel logistic regression to examine participant-specific changes and between-subject heterogeneity in the probability of engaging with each technology for more than two hours per day.'

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024), not a preprint or duplicate.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 APPLIES. Phone/SM (social media, texting, video chat, YouTube, etc.) are treated as DEPENDENT variables in analyses. Predictors are ONLY sociodemographic (biological sex, parent education, income, marital status). Analyses are LONGITUDINAL/cross-sectional (predicting technology use by baseline demographics over time from ages 9/10 to 13/14). No individual psychological/clinical predictors (no ADHD, depression, anxiety, etc.) reported. Meets all three FT-EC7 criteria (a, b, c).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 'N = 11,868 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study followed from ages ~9/10 to ~13/14'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple specific technology subtypes: social media, texting, video chat, YouTube, TV/movies, video games. Text: 'youths' self-reported hours per day (hr/day) of technology subtypes: TV/Movies, video games, YouTube, social media, video chat, and texting'

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel logistic regression examining associations between technology subtypes and sociodemographic variables (sex, parent education, income, marital status). Both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses. E.g., 'Using child-reported hours of technology use, increases in the subject-specific odds of using a technology >2 hr/day ranged from 25% (YouTube; 95% CI: 1.16-1.35) to 234% (social media; 95% CI: 3.14-3.55)'

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports OR with 95% CI for multiple associations: social media OR 3.34 (95% CI: 3.14, 3.55); TV OR 2.16 (95% CI: 2.09, 2.23); texting OR 2.22 (95% CI: 2.07, 2.39); video chat OR 1.79 (95% CI: 1.67, 1.93); YouTube OR 1.25 (95% CI: 1.16, 1.35); video games OR 2.11 (95% CI: 2.04, 2.18). Table 2 provides extensive ORs with CIs.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: 'We used multilevel logistic regression to examine participant-specific changes and between-subject heterogeneity in the probability of engaging with each technology for more than two hours per day' with longitudinal data across 5 waves

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Journal of Adolescent Health 2024

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes cross-sectional analyses with sociodemographic predictors of technology use (FT_EC7 condition (a) met for some analyses), but also includes cross-sectional data with multiple predictor types and technology use as both DV and IV in different contexts. E14 applies: sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM use with cross-sectional analysis = MET (keep INCLUDE). Cross-sectional analyses present; FT_EC7 does not apply.

title: Adolescent smartphone use, sleep, and physical activity: daily associations between sensor-based measures in the adolescent brain cognitive development (ABCD) study.
abstract: PURPOSE: Potential health consequences of adolescent smartphone use are a growing public concern. Improving upon existing, largely self-report-based research, this study investigated relationships between adolescent smartphone use, sleep, and physical activity using passive sensor measures. METHODS: Over three weeks, 791 Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study participants (Mage=14.12, 53 % female) provided smartphone application and keyboard use data via the Effortless Assessment Research System (EARS) application, and sleep and physical activity data via Fitbit device. RESULTS: Mixed-effects models found that daytime/evening application use (6:00AM-7:59PM) predicted reduced exercise, more time sedentary, and fewer daily steps (Standardized Î²=-0.21-0.07, P<.001). Late-evening use (8:00PM-9:59PM) modestly predicted increased sedentary time and reduced steps (Standardized Î²=-0.05-0.02, P<.001). Late-night use (10:00PM-5:59AM) predicted reduced sleep, delayed sleep onset, delayed waking, increased sedentary time, and fewer daily steps (Standardized Î²=-0.16-0.27, P<.001). After disaggregating within and between-person smartphone use, within-person relationships remained significant, with associations of similar magnitude to the initial analyses (daytime/evening use: standardized êµ=-0.22-0.07, P<.001; late-evening use: standardized êµ=-0.05-0.02, P<.001; late-night use: standardized êµ=-0.16-0.24, Pâ¤.002), indicating daily-level relationships unattributable to between-subject differences. Examining smartphone use effects by hour relative to sleep onset indicated that only use recorded after initial sleep onset significantly predicted sleep, while use recorded 3-12 hours before sleep onset significantly predicted step counts. CONCLUSIONS: Using passive sensor data, we found significant associations between adolescent smartphone use, physical activity, and sleep which differed by time of day and remained significant within subjects. Experimental replication is recommended to strengthen tentative causal claims.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 791 Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study participants, Mage=14.12, 53% female, with EARS passive sensing data

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone application use measured via EARS (Effortless Assessment Research System) passive sensing; reported by time window: daytime/evening (6AM-7:59PM), late-evening (8PM-9:59PM), late-night (10PM-5:59AM). Specific smartphone exposure variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models with multiple statistical tests: daytime/evening app use predicted reduced exercise, sedentary time, daily steps; late-night use predicted reduced sleep, delayed sleep onset/waking, increased sedentary time, reduced steps. P<.001 reported throughout.

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized beta coefficients reported: daytime/evening β=-0.21 to -0.07 (P<.001); late-evening β=-0.05 to -0.02 (P<.001); late-night β=-0.16 to -0.27 (P<.001). Within-person coefficients: daytime/evening β=-0.22 to -0.07 (P<.001); late-evening β=-0.05 to -0.02 (P<.001); late-night β=-0.16 to -0.24 (P≤.002).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective sensor-based study with mixed-effects models examining daily associations between smartphone use and sleep/physical activity over 3 weeks

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Psychiatry Research, 2025

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone use is independent variable; sleep, physical activity, steps are dependent variables. Cross-sectional daily-level associations. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (phone/SM as DV).

FT_IC1.evidence: Title states 'adolescent brain cognitive development (ABCD) study' and abstract absent but title explicitly indicates U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC2.evidence: Title indicates sensor-based smartphone use measures (EARS = passive sensing app categories). Sensor-based smartphone measures and app category data qualify as screen exposure variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available to verify whether daily associations reported include screen↔non-screen inferential tests (sleep and physical activity are non-screen outcomes mentioned in title). Title indicates associations but cannot confirm statistics without full-text.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; cannot verify numeric coefficients reported in results.

FT_IC5.evidence: Title indicates daily associations and sensor-based approach but no PDF to confirm study design and whether original empirical analysis of ABCD data is conducted.

FT_IC6.evidence: Appears to be peer-reviewed journal article (Psychiatry Research, 2025). Assume MET unless clear evidence of preprint/abstract/deposit status.

title: Social epidemiology of bedtime screen use behaviors and sleep outcomes in early adolescence.
abstract: OBJECTIVES: The current study aimed to determine sociodemographic associations of bedtime screen use behaviors and the sociodemographic differences in the associations between bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes in a national (US) study of early adolescents. METHODS: We analyzed cross-sectional data from 10,305 early adolescents (12-13years,Â 48.4% female) in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (Year 3, 2019-2021). Multiple regression analyses examined associations between (1) sociodemographic factors (age, sex, race and ethnicity, sexual orientation, household income, parental education, andÂ number of siblings) and adolescent-reported bedtime screen use and (2) bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes (caregiver-reported sleep disturbance and self-reported sleep duration). RESULTS: Older age, female sex, sexual minority status, lower household income, and lower parent education were associated with more bedtime screen use. Black, Native American, and Latino/Hispanic race/ethnicity were associated with more bedtime screen use compared with White race, regardless of household income or parent education. More bedtime screen use was linked to greater sleep disturbances, with stronger effects observed in male adolescents. More bedtime screen use was also associated with shorter sleep duration, particularly among female adolescents and individuals from households with higher income and parental education levels. Although sexual minority identification was associated with more bedtime screen use, it was not associated with worse sleep outcomes among these adolescents. CONCLUSIONS: Given sociodemographic differences in bedtime screen use, digital literacy education and anticipatory guidance could focus on at-risk early adolescent populations. Findings can inform targeted counseling by pediatricians and family media plans for diverse populations.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 3 (2019-2021), N=10,305 early adolescents

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use measured via self-report questionnaire covering multiple devices and modalities. Includes phone-specific items (turning off phone, using phone for texts/social media at bedtime). Weighted average of sleep duration on weekdays/weekends used to assess bedtime screen use.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression analyses examining associations between bedtime screen use (independent variable) and sleep outcomes (caregiver-reported sleep disturbance and self-reported sleep duration); also modified Poisson regression

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β and p-values reported in Table 3. Example: bedtime screen use associated with sleep disturbances (p<.001) and shorter sleep duration (p<.001) across sociodemographic subgroups

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Sleep Health, 2025

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Bedtime screen use is independent variable; sleep outcomes are dependent variable. Cross-sectional design. FT_EC7 criterion (a) requires smartphone/SM as DV in ALL analyses — criterion not met.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (N=10,305, Year 3 data, age 12-13 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use measured via 11-item questionnaire covering phone/SM use while in bed (texting, social media, video chatting, browsing); composite mean score calculated across device types

FT_IC3.evidence: Bedtime screen use is predictor of sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, sleep duration). Paper reports associations between screen use variables and sleep outcomes only. No phone/SM as independent variable linked to non-SM behavioral/clinical outcome; sleep is physical/health outcome, not a separate category meeting the 'at least one non-SM variable' criterion in the context of directionality analysis

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC3 NOT_MET means numeric reporting not evaluable for phone/SM association criterion

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD cross-sectional analysis (Year 3); empirical analysis with regression models

C2.FT_EC7_reason: All analyses report bedtime screen use as independent variable predicting sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, sleep duration). No non-SM/non-sleep outcomes reported. Sleep is physiological outcome. Bedtime screen use could be analyzed as outcome in relation to sociodemographic predictors. However, per FT-IC3 criterion, the main hypothesis structure focuses on screen use → sleep, making this a screen-outcome study rather than smartphone/SM → behavioral/psychological outcome study. Applies E7 logic but FT-IC3 already excludes based on directionality of exposure-outcome framing.
